subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is connected to a battery of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> A dielectric slab of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> is now inserted into the space between plates of capacitor while the battery remain connected. The change in the electrostatic energy in the capacitor will be __________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is connected to a battery of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> A dielectric slab of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> is now inserted into the space between plates of capacitor while the battery remain connected. The change in the electrostatic energy in the capacitor will be __________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Initially
C=200μF
Ei=12CV2=12×200×10-6×2002
Finally
C'=KC=400μF
Ef=12C'V2=12×400×10-6×(200)2
ΔE=12×400-200×10-6×4×104=4 J

question: The charge on capacitor of capacitance 15μF in the figure given below is

subject: Physics
q:
The charge accumulated on the capacitor connected in the following circuit is ______μC.

(Given C=150 μF)
question:
The charge accumulated on the capacitor connected in the following circuit is ______μC.

(Given C=150 μF)
solution:
Under the balanced condition, the capacitor will act as an open circuit.

The current i1 through the 1 Ω resistor is given by

i1=10 V1 Ω+2 Ω=103 A

The current i2 through the 6 Ω resistor is given by

i2=10 V6 Ω+4 Ω=1 A

From the above diagram, it can be written that

VA+i1×1-i2×6=VB⇒VA-VB=1×6-103×1=83 V

Hence, the charge on the capacitor is given by

Q=CVA-VB=150 μF×83 V=400 μC

given ϵ0=8.86×10-12C2/Nm2
solution:

=VdAkε0Akε0=VCAkε0∵kAε0d=C
106=500×15×10-1210-4×k×8.85×10-12
k=15×5008.85×10-4×106=758.85
k=8.47≈8.5

q: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation d=2 m has a capacitance of 4 μF. The new capacitance of the system if half of the space between them is filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K=3 (as shown in figure) will be
question: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation d=2 m has a capacitance of 4 μF. The new capacitance of the system if half of the space between them is filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K=3 (as shown in figure) will be
solution:
This parallel plate capacitor can be divided into two capacitors. One with dielectric C1 and other without dielectricC2. The two capacitors will be in series.

Initially C=ε0Ad=4 μF

Now, C1=kε0Ad2=2×3×ε0Ad=24 μF and C2=ε0Ad2=2×ε0Ad=8 μF

Finally C'=C1C2C1+C2=24×824+8=6 μF

q:
In the given circuit.C1=2μF,C2=0.2μF,C3=2μF,C4=4μF,C5=
2μF,C6=2μF. The charge stored on capacitor C4 is  ______   μC.
question:

2μF,C6=2μF. The charge stored on capacitor C4 is  ______   μC.
solution:
From the diagram of the circuit, C3, C4, C5 are in series, so the total capacitance is 

1C'=12+14+12⇒C'=45 μF

C' & C2 are parallel, the total capacitance is 

C''=45+0.2 μF=1 μF

C1, C'', C6 are in series. So the equivalent capacitance is,

1Ceq=12+1+12⇒Ceq=0.5 μF

Using Q=CeqV.

The charge through the battery, Q =10×12=5 μC

 From the diagram above,

Q'=5μC×0.80.8+0.2=4 μC

subject: Physics
q: Two capacitors having capacitance C1 and C2 respectively are connected as shown in figure. Initially, capacitor C1 is charged to a potential difference V volt by a battery. The battery is then removed and the charged capacitor C1 is now connected to uncharged capacitor C2 by closing the switch S. The amount of charge on the capacitor C2, after equilibrium, is
question: Two capacitors having capacitance C1 and C2 respectively are connected as shown in figure. Initially, capacitor C1 is charged to a potential difference V volt by a battery. The battery is then removed and the charged capacitor C1 is now connected to uncharged capacitor C2 by closing the switch S. The amount of charge on the capacitor C2, after equilibrium, is
solution:
The initial charge on the capacitor C1 will be Q=C1V. If after closing the  switch the charge flown through the circuit is q, the charge on C2 will be q and C1 will be Q-q. The potential across the capacitors will be same. Hence,

Q-qC1=qC2⇒q=C2QC1+C2⇒q=C1C2C1+C2V

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $12.5 \mathrm{pF}$ is charged by a battery connected between its plates to potential difference of $12.0 \mathrm{~V}$. The battery is now disconnected and a dielectric slab $\left(\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=6\right)$ is inserted between the plates. The change in its potential energy after inserting the dielectric slab is _____ $10^{-12} \mathrm{~J}$.
question: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $12.5 \mathrm{pF}$ is charged by a battery connected between its plates to potential difference of $12.0 \mathrm{~V}$. The battery is now disconnected and a dielectric slab $\left(\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=6\right)$ is inserted between the plates. The change in its potential energy after inserting the dielectric slab is _____ $10^{-12} \mathrm{~J}$.
solution:
Before inserting dielectric capacitance is given $\mathrm{C}_0=12.5 \mathrm{pF}$ and charge on the capacitor $\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}$ After inserting dielectric capacitance will become $\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_0$. Change in potential energy of the capacitor $\begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_0}\left[1-\frac{1}{\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}\right] \\ & =\frac{\left(\mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}\right)^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_0}\left[1-\frac{1}{\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}\right]=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}^2\left[1-\frac{1}{\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}\right] \end{aligned}$
Using $\mathrm{C}_0=12.5 \mathrm{pF}, \mathrm{V}=12 \mathrm{~V}, \epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=6$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}(12.5) \times 12^2\left[1-\frac{1}{6}\right]=\frac{1}{2}(12.5) \times 12^2 \times \frac{5}{6} \\ & =750 \mathrm{pJ}=750 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor has charge $5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$. A dielectric slab is inserted between the plates and almost fills the space between the plates. If the induced charge on one face of the slab is $4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$ then the dielectric constant of the slab is ________.
question: A parallel plate capacitor has charge $5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$. A dielectric slab is inserted between the plates and almost fills the space between the plates. If the induced charge on one face of the slab is $4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$ then the dielectric constant of the slab is ________.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{in}}=\mathrm{Q}\left(1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}\right) \\ & 4 \times 10^{-6}=5 \times 10^{-6}\left(1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}\right) \\ & 1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}=\frac{4}{5} \\ & \mathrm{~K}=5\end{aligned}$

412C1V2=12C2V2
⇒  4C1=C2...(ii)
From equation (i) and (ii)
C1=2μF
C2=8μF
If they are in series

each capacitance C1=ε0A3 before

question:

solution:
The dielectric strength given is 3.6×107 V m-1.and the separation is 1 mm. Therefore, maximum potential difference that can be applied will be,

Vmax=3.6×107×d.

Now,

C=qmaxVmax⇒kε0Ad=7×10-73.6×107×dk=dε0×30π×10-4×7×10-73.6×107×d=2.33

question: The equivalent capacitance between points A and B in below shown figure will be _____ μF.

Capacitors between points A and 3 are in parallel combination as shown above and their combined capacitance value will be 3C.

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit diagram, when the current reaches a steady-state in the circuit, the charge on the capacitor of capacitance C will be:
question: In the given circuit diagram, when the current reaches a steady-state in the circuit, the charge on the capacitor of capacitance C will be:
solution:
In steady state, current through capacitor will be zero, so current through r1 will be zero.

Hence, the net current flowing in the circuit will be,
I=Er+r2
The potential difference across the capacitor will be,
V2=I×r2=Er2r+r2

Therefore, charge on the capacitor plate will be,
q=CV2=CEr2r+r2

solution:
∵1 μF & 2 μF are in parallel.



∴  The equivalent capacitance of the series combination is, Ceq is=3CC+3. So, the total charge supplied by the battery is, 
⇒Q=CeqE=3CEC+3.∴ The potential difference across the parallel combination of 1 μF and 2μF is, ∆V=Q3=CEC+3. So charge on 2 μF capacitor is, Q2=C2∆V=2CEC+3⇒Q22E=CC+3⇒Q22E=C+3-3C+3

⇒Q22E=1-3C+3⇒ So the graph is a hyperbola. With downward curve line. i.e.

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate air capacitor is connected to a battery. The plates are pulled apart at uniform speed $v$. If $x$ is the separation between the plates at any instant, then the time rate of change of electrostatic energy of the capacitor is proportional to $x^\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is _____.
question: A parallel plate air capacitor is connected to a battery. The plates are pulled apart at uniform speed $v$. If $x$ is the separation between the plates at any instant, then the time rate of change of electrostatic energy of the capacitor is proportional to $x^\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is _____.
solution:
The electrostatic energy of a parallel plate capacitor is given by $U = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$.

For a parallel plate capacitor, the capacitance is $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x}$, where $x$ is the separation between the plates.

Since the capacitor remains connected to the battery, the potential difference $V$ across the plates is constant.

Substituting $C$ into the energy equation gives:

$U = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x}$

Differentiating $U$ with respect to time $t$ to find the time rate of change of electrostatic energy:

$\dfrac{dU}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x} \right) = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x^2} \dfrac{dx}{dt}$

Given that the plates are pulled apart at a uniform speed $v$, we have $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = v$.

$\dfrac{dU}{dt} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2 v}{2x^2}$

From the above expression, it is clear that $\dfrac{dU}{dt} \propto x^{-2}$.

Comparing this with $x^\alpha$, we get $\alpha = -2$.

Answer: $-2$

By energy conservation, it follows that

12CV2=12LImax2   ...1

Imax=CLV=100×10-66.4×10-3×12=128=1.5 A

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor is filled equally (half) with two dielectrics of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, as shown in figures. The distance between the plates is d and area of each plate is A. If capacitance in first configuration and second configuration are $C_1$ and $C_2$ respectively, then $\frac{C_1}{C_2}$ is :
question: A parallel plate capacitor is filled equally (half) with two dielectrics of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, as shown in figures. The distance between the plates is d and area of each plate is A. If capacitance in first configuration and second configuration are $C_1$ and $C_2$ respectively, then $\frac{C_1}{C_2}$ is :
solution:
Area of plate is $A$. then
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}} \\ & \mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}
\end{aligned}$
Let $\mathrm{C}_0=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}=2 \varepsilon_2 \mathrm{C}_0 \\ & \mathrm{C}^{\prime}=2 \varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{C} \& \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ are in series
$\mathrm{C}_1=\frac{\mathrm{CC}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{C}^{\prime}}=\frac{4 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_0\left(\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_1\right)}$
$=\frac{2 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0}{\left(\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_1\right)}$
Here $\mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \mathrm{~d}}=\frac{\varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0}{2}$
$\mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon_2 \mathrm{C}_0}{2}$
$\mathrm{C} \& \mathrm{C}^{\prime}$ are inparallel
$\mathrm{C}_2=\mathrm{C}^{\prime}+\mathrm{C}=\left(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2\right) \frac{\mathrm{C}_0}{2}$
Thus $\frac{\mathrm{C}_1}{\mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{2 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_1 \mathrm{C}_0}{\left(\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_1\right)} \times \frac{2}{\left(\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2\right) \mathrm{C}_0}$
$=\frac{4 \varepsilon_2 \varepsilon_1}{\left(\varepsilon_2+\varepsilon_1\right)^2}$

question: The capacitor of an oscillatory circuit is enclosed in a container. When the container is evacuated, the resonance frequency of the circuit is $10 \mathrm{kHz}$. q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is fully charged to a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> After removing the source voltage it is connected to an uncharged capacitor in parallel. Now the potential difference across them becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The capacitance of the second capacitor is :
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is fully charged to a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> After removing the source voltage it is connected to an uncharged capacitor in parallel. Now the potential difference across them becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The capacitance of the second capacitor is :
solution:
V=C1V1+C2V2C1+C2
20=10×50+020+C
C=15μF

subject: Physics
q: The equivalent capacitance of the combination shown is
question: The equivalent capacitance of the combination shown is
solution:
The potential difference across the branches EF & GH is zero. So, the equivalent capacitance will be only due to the combination of two capacitors in parallel.

Ceq=C+C

⇒Ceq=2 C

question: For the given input voltage waveform Vin (t), the output voltage waveform V0(t), across the capacitor is correctly depicted by :
solution:
When first pulse is applied the potential across capacitor is given by V=V01-e-tRC

When no pulse is applied, capacitor will discharge like V=V0'et/RC when again, second pulse is applied V=V01-e-tRC

Using all three equations the correct graph is :

subject: Physics
q:
A parallel plate capacitor whose capacitance C is 14 pF is charged by a battery to a potential difference V=12 V between its plates. The charging battery is now disconnected and a porcelain plate with k=7 is inserted between the plates, then the plate would oscillate back and forth between the plates with a constant mechanical energy of _______ pJ.

(Assume no friction)
question:
A parallel plate capacitor whose capacitance C is 14 pF is charged by a battery to a potential difference V=12 V between its plates. The charging battery is now disconnected and a porcelain plate with k=7 is inserted between the plates, then the plate would oscillate back and forth between the plates with a constant mechanical energy of _______ pJ.

(Assume no friction)
solution:
Ui=12×14×12×12 pJ  ∵U=12CV2

=1008 pJ

Uf=10087pJ=144 pJ  ∵Cm=kC0

Mechanical energy=ΔU

=1008-144

=864 pJ

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor with plate separation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is charged up by a battery. It is found that on introducing a dielectric sheet of thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, while keeping the battery connections intact, the capacitor draws <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> more charge from the battery than before. The dielectric constant of the sheet is ____.
question: A parallel plate capacitor with plate separation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> is charged up by a battery. It is found that on introducing a dielectric sheet of thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, while keeping the battery connections intact, the capacitor draws <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> more charge from the battery than before. The dielectric constant of the sheet is ____.
solution:
Without dielectric, capacitance will be C=Aε0d.

Therefore, charge on it, Q=Aε0dV

Now, with dielectric capacitance will be C'=Aε0d-t+tK.

Hence, charge on it will be Q'=Aε0Vd-t+tK

Given:

Aε0Vd-t+tK=1.25Aε0Vd

⇒1.253+2K=5

⇒K=2

subject: Physics
q:
Two circuits (a) and (b) have charged capacitors of capacitance C, 2C and 3C with open switches. Charges on each of the capacitor are as shown in the figures. On closing the switches

Circuit (a) Circuit (b)
question:
Two circuits (a) and (b) have charged capacitors of capacitance C, 2C and 3C with open switches. Charges on each of the capacitor are as shown in the figures. On closing the switches

Circuit (a) Circuit (b)
options: No charge flows in (a) but charge flows from $R$ to $L$ in (b)
Charges flow from $L$ to $R$ in both (a) and (b)
Charges flow from $R$ to $L$ in (a) and from $L$ to $R$ in (b)
No charge flows in (a) but charge flows from $L$ to $R$ in (b)
solution:
Charge (or current) always flows from higher potential to lower potential.
Potential $=\frac{\text { Charge }}{\text { Capacitance }}$
